function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

messages.content:
Read and summarize the FirmVault case workflow system. I need the complete picture of how cases are SUPPOSED to work according to the formal system.

Read these files and extract the full workflow specification:
1. /opt/data/FirmVault/skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml - the phase definitions, landmarks, predicates
2. /opt/data/FirmVault/DESIGN.md - architectural design
3. /opt/data/FirmVault/skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md - data contract
4. /opt/data/FirmVault/skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md - runtime system
5. /opt/data/FirmVault/skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md - task schema
6. /opt/data/FirmVault/Templates/INDEX.md - template library
7. List all skills in /opt/data/FirmVault/skills.tools.workflows/Skills/ and read the SKILL.md for the top 10-15 most important ones

Output a structured summary covering:
- All phases and their landmarks/gates
- The prescribed workflow for each phase (what's supposed to happen)
- Skills available per phase
- Templates available per phase
- Automation/runtime expectations
- Any explicit rules, constraints, or SLAs mentioned

Write the full summary to /tmp/firmvault_workflow_spec.md

messages.reasoning: Let me start by reading all the key files in parallel, and listing the skills directory.

{"content": "     1|# Phase DAG — machine-readable case progression
     2|#
     3|# This file is the authoritative definition of the case lifecycle:
     4|# every phase, every landmark, what gates phase transitions, and
     5|# which workflows produce which landmark.
     6|#
     7|# A materializer (see skills.tools.workflows/runtime/) reads this
     8|# file together with the vault and emits actionable Tasks. A
     9|# reconciler reads this file plus the vault and computes which
    10|# landmarks are now satisfied. The narrative phase READMEs and
    11|# landmarks.md files are documentation; this YAML is the contract.
    12|#
    13|# Conditions are expressed as small predicates the materializer
    14|# evaluates against a Case object loaded from cases/<slug>/<slug>.md
    15|# per skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md.
    16|#
    17|# Predicate vocabulary (Python-ish, evaluated with safe_eval):
    18|#   case.status == "intake"                  # frontmatter status
    19|#   case.frontmatter.<key> == <value>        # any frontmatter key
    20|#   case.has_document("hipaa")               # filename pattern in cases/<slug>/documents/
    21|#   case.has_section("Insurance Claims")     # markdown heading present
    22|#   case.section_bullets("Insurance Claims") > 0
    23|#   case.contacts(role="treating_provider").count > 0
    24|#   case.claims.count > 0
    25|#   case.liens.where(status="outstanding").count == 0
    26|#   case.activity.last("client_contact").age_days < 30
    27|#   all(p.records_requested for p in case.providers)
    28|#   any(p.records_received for p in case.providers)
    29|#   case.flag("attorney_approved_demand")    # bool flag in frontmatter
    30|#
    31|# Authors: write predicates that touch ONLY the vault. Anything that
    32|# would require a database, an API call, or external state belongs in
    33|# a Task with a worker, not in a predicate.
    34|
    35|schema_version: 1
    36|
    37|phases:
    38|
    39|  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    40|  phase_0_onboarding:
    41|    name: Onboarding
    42|    description: >
    43|      Initial client retention. Three signed documents must be on file
    44|      before any substantive work begins.
    45|    entry_triggers:
    46|      - new_case_created
    47|    landmarks:

    49|        name: Client Info Received

    61|            step: receive_signed_contract
    62|      - id: medical_auth_signed

    65|        condition: case.has_document("hipaa") or case.has_document("medical authorization")
    66|        produced_by:
    67|          - workflow: document_collection
    68|            step: receive_signed_hipaa
    69|    exit_blockers:
    70|      hard: [client_info_received, contract_signed, medical_auth_signed]
    71|      soft: []
    72|    transitions:

    85|    entry_triggers:
    86|      - phase_0_onboarding.exited
    87|    landmarks:

    89|        name: Full Intake Complete

    91|        condition: case.frontmatter.intake_complete == true

# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

   149|- **UI / dashboard** — gives the operator visibility into queue depth, in-flight tasks, failures, cost. Track A requires this as a hard success criterion.
   150|
   151|### 2.2 The five candidate architectures
   152|
   153|Each architecture is a scaffolded reference prototype under `experiments/`. None of them have been deployed yet. Each was built by a parallel subagent following a self-contained brief.
   154|
   155|| # | Architecture | `experiments/` path | Files / lines |
   156||---|---|---|---|
   157|| **1** | **Pure GitHub-native** (`gh aw` + Issues + Projects v2 + PRs) | `arch-1-pure-github/` | 6 / ~1,001 |
   158|| **2** | **Vibe Kanban** (REST API + per-task worktrees + diff review) | `arch-2-vibe-kanban/` | 5 / ~1,290 |
   159|| **3** | **Mission Control** (SQLite + Aegis reviewer + queue-poll dispatch) | `arch-3-mission-control/` | 6 / ~1,070 |
   160|| **4** | **Custom Case Cockpit** (Next.js + Drizzle + Postgres + case-centric UI) | `arch-4-case-cockpit/` | 28 / ~2,076 |
   161|| **5** | **Claude Code `schedule` skill** (three scheduled prompts + GitHub) | `arch-5-schedule-skill/` | 7 / ~921 |
   162|
   163|### 2.3 Role-by-architecture mapping
   164|
   165|Same seven roles, five architectures. Read a column to see how one architecture assigns components to roles; read a row to see how the same role is implemented differently across architectures.
   166|
   167|| Role | Arch 1 Pure GitHub | Arch 2 Vibe Kanban | Arch 3 Mission Control | Arch 4 Case Cockpit | Arch 5 schedule skill |
   168||---|---|---|---|---|---|
   169|| Materializer | `gh aw` cron workflow (`materializer.md`) | `materializer.py` stdlib script (cron'd on Render or locally) | `materializer.py` stdlib + PyYAML (cron in MC blueprint every 10m) | `src/lib/materializer.ts` + `/api/materialize` route (manual or cron) | Scheduled Claude agent running `materializer-prompt.md` |

   171|| Dispatcher | `gh aw` worker workflow, bash `case` on CLI | VK native (bundled executor) | Queue-poll via `worker-shell-hook.sh` | `src/lib/worker.ts` via `child_process.spawn` | Scheduled Claude agent running `worker-prompt.md` |
   172|| Runner | GitHub Actions runner (shells out to `claude` / `codex` / `gemini`) | VK-managed git worktree in its container | Host running the shell hook (wherever that is) | Next.js server runtime (wherever the app runs) | Claude Code's scheduled-agent runtime (likely managed by Anthropic) |

   174|| Review gate | GitHub PR review | VK's diff view + merge button (can also open GH PR) | Aegis auto-reviewer **then** GitHub PR review | GitHub PR (or dashboard button, MVP = PR) | GitHub PR review |
   175|| Landmark updater | `gh aw` workflow on `pull_request.closed` (`landmark-detector.md`) | Post-merge git hook or polling materializer | Next materializer tick | Next materializer tick | Scheduled Claude agent running `landmark-detector-prompt.md` |

   177|### 2.4 Comparison dimensions
   178|
   179|The bake-off is trying to answer specific questions. These are the dimensions I'll use to score each arch after it runs.
   180|
   181|| Dimension | Arch 1 | Arch 2 | Arch 3 | Arch 4 | Arch 5 |
   182||---|---|---|---|---|---|
   183|| Infra to deploy | None | One Render service | One Render service | One web service + managed Postgres | None |
   184|| Multi-agent dispatch (hard requirement) | ✅ Bash shell-out to all three CLIs | ✅ Native | ⚠ Queue-poll + shell hook (works, non-trivial) | ✅ `child_process.spawn` | ❌ Very likely Claude-only |
   185|| Review gate | GitHub PR (strong) | VK diff view (strong) | Aegis LLM review (weak — narrative only) + PR (strong) | GitHub PR (strong) | GitHub PR (strong) |
   186|| Observability / UI | GitHub Projects v2 (functional, not great) | VK kanban (good) | MC's 32 panels (strongest) | Custom case-centric pages (tailored) | GitHub Issues only (weakest) |
   187|| Setup friction | Low: install `gh aw`, compile, set secrets | Medium: deploy VK, configure API key, register repo | Medium-high: deploy MC, register agents, configure Aegis SOUL | High: deploy app + DB, configure env vars | Low: three scheduled commands |
   188|| Time to first green run | ~30 min | ~2 hours | ~3 hours | ~4 hours | ~15 min |
   189|| Runtime cost per month (steady state) | $0 + Actions minutes + agent tokens | ~$7 Render Standard + agent tokens | ~$7 Render Starter + agent tokens | ~$14 Render (app + DB) + agent tokens | $0 + agent tokens (likely higher — polling burns) |
   190|| Agent lock-in risk | Low (any CLI) | Low (any CLI VK supports) | Medium (MC dispatcher surface is narrower) | Low (any CLI you can spawn) | **High** (Claude only) |
   191|| Vault source-of-truth purity | ✅ Strict | ⚠ VK holds some metadata alongside | ⚠ MC holds task + audit in SQLite | ⚠ Postgres holds queue + audit | ✅ Strict |
   192|| Fits "firm as code repo" thesis | 10/10 | 6/10 | 5/10 | 6/10 | 9/10 |
   193|| Fits "multi-experiment testing orchestration pattern" goal | 8/10 | 7/10 | 9/10 | 5/10 | 6/10 |
   194|
   195|Scores are opinionated, not empirical. The pilot will produce evidence.
   196|
   197|### 2.5 Architecture-specific findings from the build
   198|
   199|Things the subagents discovered while building, worth remembering before the pilot:
   200|
   201|- **Arch 1**: `gh aw`'s exact frontmatter schema (`engine`, `tools`, `with`, `concurrency`) is not fully verified. First `gh aw compile` run will probably surface schema errors that need fixing. CLI flags for `claude --print` / `codex exec` / `gemini --prompt` are plausible guesses that will also need tweaking once something actually runs.
   202|- **Arch 2**: Vibe Kanban's real API endpoint is **`POST /api/workspaces/start`**, not `/api/tasks` — `/api/tasks` was deprecated/renamed. Verified by reading the VK Rust source (`crates/server/src/routes/workspaces/mod.rs`). The subagent corrected the materializer to use the right endpoint. Seven other uncertainties about VK's internals are flagged in `experiments/arch-2-vibe-kanban/firmvault-vk-integration.md §6`.
   203|- **Arch 3**: Mission Control has **no `/api/tasks/<id>/resolution` endpoint** despite what the brief assumed. Resolutions are written via `PUT /api/tasks/<id>` with `{resolution, status}`. Verified from MC source. Also: MC's Aegis reviewer **only sees the first 6000 chars of the worker's resolution text, not the diff** — which is a significant weakness for paralegal work where the artifact is the deliverable.
   204|- **Arch 4**: The worker has a **deterministic fallback path for `write-case-summary`** — the bake-off smoke test passes even without an agent CLI installed, which lets you prove the architecture works end-to-end without burning API tokens. Clever touch.
   205|- **Arch 5**: The subagent was **honest about this architecture's weaknesses** — `schedule` is almost certainly Claude-only (failing Track A criterion #1), has the weakest observability, burns the most tokens per unit of useful work (polling, no event-drivenness), creates agent lock-in, and has no webhook story for the landmark detector. Its only honest win is "speed to first working loop" — a legitimate reason to build it as a baseline before porting prompts into a stronger architecture.
   206|
   207|### 2.6 Recommendation: which to pilot first, and in what order
   208|
   209|**Pilot order**: 1 → 2, then decide.
   210|
   211|**Arch 1 first** because:
   212|
   213|- Zero deployment cost — everything lives inside the repo and GitHub
   214|- Fastest way to validate whether the `write-case-summary` test harness actually works end-to-end with a real agent
   215|- Tests the critical `gh aw` multi-agent dispatch assumption that everything else is trying to work around
   216|- If Arch 1 works, it's likely "good enough" for the orchestration-pattern test the user mentioned — most of the wanted features are already there
   217|- If Arch 1 fails, the failure teaches us exactly what Arch 2/3/4 need to replace
   218|
   219|**Arch 2 second** because:
   220|
   221|- If Arch 1 works, Arch 2 is the obvious UX upgrade — native multi-agent in worktrees plus a real diff review experience is a stronger product story than GitHub Projects
   222|- One deployment on Render — small but real infra cost, worth testing separately
   223|- Exercises the "the materializer is a thin bridge, the UI is someone else's code" hypothesis, which is worth knowing for the product pitch
   224|- The subagent verified the VK API shape against source, so the materializer should work on first run
   225|
   226|**Arch 3 (Mission Control) third**, conditionally: pilot only if Arch 1 and Arch 2 both succeed AND we want to evaluate MC's observability panels and Aegis gate as additive layers, not as the primary runtime. Mission Control's strongest pitch is the dashboard, not the dispatcher.
   227|
   228|**Arch 4 (Custom Case Cockpit) fourth**, conditionally: pilot only if none of Arch 1/2/3 deliver a good case-centric UI experience. Building a custom UI has significant opportunity cost vs. using one of the existing tools.
   229|
   230|**Arch 5 (schedule skill) as baseline**: build and smoke-test it **alongside Arch 1** because it's effectively free — same 15-minute setup, same task template, same GitHub Issues store, just different dispatch and cadence. If Arch 5 fires correctly and Arch 1 doesn't, we have a fallback. If both work, we have a concrete comparison between `gh aw` and `schedule` as schedulers.

{"content": "     1|# Data Contract — FirmVault Paralegal System
     2|
     3|This document is the **single source of truth** for where each piece of case data lives in the FirmVault Obsidian vault. Every skill, tool, and workflow in this directory MUST read and write case data through these paths. Do not invent new locations.
     4|
     5|> **Important:** The system used to back state in a FalkorDB graph. That is no longer the case. The Obsidian vault is the only state store. References to FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`, or per-case JSON files (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`) are deprecated and being removed.
     6|
     7|## 1. Repository layout
     8|
     9|```
    10|FirmVault/                                  # repo root, also the Obsidian vault root
    11|├── cases/<case-slug>/                      # one folder per matter
    12|│   ├── <case-slug>.md                      # CANONICAL case file (frontmatter + sections)
    13|│   ├── Dashboard.md                        # Obsidian dataview dashboard
    14|│   ├── claims/<type>-<carrier-slug>.md     # one file per insurance claim
    15|│   ├── liens/<creditor-slug>.md            # one file per lien
    16|│   ├── contacts/<entity-slug>.md           # per-case contact stubs (link to master cards)
    17|│   ├── documents/                          # case-specific docs (PDFs, drafts, signed forms)
    18|│   └── Activity Log/<YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM>-<category>.md   # one file per logged activity
    19|├── Contacts/
    20|│   ├── Clients/<slug>.md                   # client master cards
    21|│   ├── Insurance/<slug>.md                 # carriers AND adjusters (mixed; entity_subtype distinguishes)
    22|│   ├── Medical/<slug>.md                   # medical provider master cards
    23|│   ├── Legal/, Liens/, courts/, judges/, attorneys/, lien-holders/, medical-providers/, other/
    24|├── Templates/<slug>.{docx,pdf}             # firm template library (intake, complaints, demands, etc.)
    25|├── skills.tools.workflows/                 # the paralegal system itself (this folder)
    26|└── Firm Dashboard.md                       # firm-wide rollup
    27|```
    28|
    29|## 2. The case file: `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md`
    30|
    31|This is the canonical record for a matter. Every other file in `cases/<slug>/` either renders or extends it.
    32|
    33|### Required frontmatter
    34|
    35|```yaml
    36|---
    37|schema_version: 2
    38|case_id: <uuid>                  # stable identifier; do not change
    39|client_name: <Full Name>
    40|case_type: auto_accident | premises | wc | wd | med_mal | other
    41|status: intake | file_setup | treatment | demand | negotiation | settlement | lien | litigation | closed
    42|date_of_incident: "YYYY-MM-DD"
    43|jurisdiction: KY | IN | other    # optional
    44|case_number: <free-form id>      # optional
    45|---
    46|```
    47|
    48|### Required sections (order matters; agents grep for these headings)
    49|
    50|```markdown
    51|# <Client Name> — <case_type> <date_of_incident>
    52|
    53|**Status:** <status> | **Type:** <case_type> | **Jurisdiction:** <jurisdiction>
    54|
    55|## Medical Providers
    56|- [[contacts/<provider-slug>|<Provider Name>]]
    57|<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->
    58|### Treatment Details (Roscoe)
    59|| Provider | Status | Start | End | Billed | Bills Req | Bills Rec | Records Req | Records Rec |
    60|<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->
    61|
    62|## Adjusters
    63|- [[contacts/<adjuster-slug>|<Adjuster Name>]]
    64|
    65|## Insurance Claims
    66|- [[claims/<type>-<carrier-slug>|<Type> — <Carrier>]]
    67|<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->
    68|### Insurance Details (Roscoe)
    69|| Type | Carrier | Claim # | Adjuster | Adjuster Phone | Adjuster Email | Carrier Phone | Carrier Address |
    70|<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->
    71|
    72|## Liens
    73|- [[liens/<creditor-slug>|<Creditor>]] — <type> (<status>)
    74|
    75|## Expenses
    76|| Creditor | Amount | Category |
    77|
    78|## Activity Log
    79|Recent: [[Activity Log/...]]
    80|```
    81|
    82|The `<!-- roscoe-*-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-*-end -->` markers are managed by the import scripts and will be replaced on every sync. **Anything between the markers is overwritten.** Do not edit by hand.
    83|
    84|## 3. Where each piece of state lives
    85|
    86|| Question agents need answered | Read from | Write to |
    87||---|---|---|
    88|| What phase is this case in? | `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md` frontmatter `status` | Same |
    89|| What landmarks have been hit? | Frontmatter `landmarks: {…}` (boolean map) | Same |
    90|| Who is the client? | Frontmatter `client_name` + `Contacts/Clients/<slug>.md` | Both |
    91|| What insurance does the case have? | `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md` `## Insurance Claims` + `cases/<slug>/claims/*.md` | New file in `claims/` + bullet in case file |
    92|| Who are the adjusters? | `cases/<slug>/contacts/<adjuster-slug>.md` linking to `Contacts/Insurance/<slug>.md` | Same |
    93|| Who are the medical providers? | `cases/<slug>/contacts/<provider-slug>.md` linking to `Contacts/Medical/<slug>.md` | Same |
    94|| What's the treatment status? | `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` table in case file | Replaced wholesale by importer |
    95|| What liens exist? | `cases/<slug>/liens/*.md` + `## Liens` section | New file + bullet |
    96|| What expenses are tracked? | `## Expenses` table in case file | Append row |
    97|| What was logged today? | `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/<YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM>-<category>.md` | New file per event |
    98|| What documents are on file? | `cases/<slug>/documents/` (filesystem listing) | Drop files there |
    99|| What template do I use for X? | `Templates/<slug>.docx` (or `.pdf`) | Read-only |
   100|
   101|## 4. Slug rules
   102|
   103|All slugs throughout the vault follow the same convention:
   104|
   105|- Lowercase
   106|- Apostrophes and curly quotes stripped (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
   107|- `&` → `and`
   108|- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
   109|- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped
   110|
   111|Examples: `Allstate Insurance` → `allstate-insurance`, `K'daya Austin` → `kdaya-austin`, `River City Orthopedics & Sports Medicine` → `river-city-orthopedics-and-sports-medicine`.
   112|
   113|## 5. Activity log entry format
   114|
   115|Filename: `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/<YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM>-<category>[-N].md`. Categories agents may use: `correspondence`, `legal`, `phone`, `meeting`, `imported`, `system`. The `-N` suffix disambiguates multiple entries within the same minute.
   116|
   117|Frontmatter:
   118|
   119|```yaml
   120|---
   121|schema_version: 2
   122|case_id: <uuid>                  # mirrors the parent case file
   123|date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
   124|time: "HH:MM:SS"
   125|category: <category>
   126|subcategory: <free-form>
   127|---
   128|```
   129|
   130|Body always opens with the wikilink back to the case:
   131|
   132|```markdown
   133|# <category> — <date>
   134|
   135|**Case:** [[cases/<slug>/<slug>|<Client Name>]]
   136|
   137|<note body>
   138|```
   139|
   140|## 6. Templates
   141|
   142|The firm template library lives in `Templates/<slug>.{docx,pdf}` at the repo root. Templates are read-only firm assets. Skills that fill templates should:
   143|
   144|1. Read the template from `Templates/<slug>.docx`.
   145|2. Write the filled output to `cases/<slug>/documents/<descriptive-name>.docx`.
   146|3. Never modify the source template.
   147|
   148|A template index lives at `Templates/INDEX.md` (to be created in a follow-up commit) listing each template, its purpose, and the placeholders it expects.
   149|
   150|## 7. What is NOT in the vault
   151|
   152|To keep the contract honest, the following are explicitly **not** stored in the vault and skills should not look for them:
   153|
   154|- Per-case JSON state files (`overview.json`, etc.) — deleted, do not recreate
   155|- A FalkorDB graph instance — removed
   156|- A `${ROSCOE_ROOT}` environment variable — removed; everything is repo-relative
   157|- Proprietary Filevine internal IDs beyond what's already imported into activity log notes
   158|- Scanned medical records, billing PDFs from providers — those go in `cases/<slug>/documents/`, not in the vault metadata
   159|
   160|## 8. Reading and writing rules for agents
   161|
   162|1. **Reads** are cheap. Always read `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md` first to ground yourself in the case before any action.
   163|2. **Writes** must preserve frontmatter and section ordering. When in doubt, edit existing sections rather than appending new ones.
   164|3. **Never** edit content between `<!-- roscoe-*-start -->` and `<!-- roscoe-*-end -->` markers — those get overwritten by syncs.
   165|4. **Slugify** any new filename using the rules in §4. Mismatched slugs break wikilinks.
   166|5. **Wikilinks** use the form `[[cases/<slug>/<slug>|<Display Name>]]` for case files, `[[contacts/<slug>|<Display Name>]]` for per-case contact stubs, `[[Contacts/Insurance/<slug>|<Display Name>]]` for master cards.
   167|6. **Commits** should describe the agent action and the case affected. The git history is part of the audit trail.
   168|
   169|## 9. When this contract changes
   170|
   171|Any change to vault layout, frontmatter schemas, or section conventions MUST be reflected here in the same commit that introduces the change. Agents and skills consult this file as authoritative; drift between this doc and reality is the bug.

{"content": "     1|# Runtime — turning the workflow system into something that drives itself
     2|
     3|This folder is the **active layer** of the paralegal system. The phase definitions in `../workflows/`, the skills in `../Skills/`, and the templates in `../../Templates/` describe *what* can be done. The runtime answers *what should be done now, on which case, by whom*.
     4|
     5|## The model in 60 seconds
     6|
     7|```
     8|                ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
     9|                │                vault (truth)                  │
    10|                │  cases/<slug>/<slug>.md, dashboards, contacts │
    11|                └───────────────┬─────────────────┬─────────────┘
    12|                                │                 │
    13|                  reads phase   │                 │  reads tasks
    14|                                ▼                 ▼
    15|              ┌─────────────────────────┐   ┌────────────────────┐
    16|   cron       │      MATERIALIZER       │   │       WORKER       │   triggered
    17|   every  ─▶  │  · evaluate landmarks   │   │  · pick ready task │  ◀─ by label
    18|   15 min     │  · for unsatisfied:     │   │  · load skill      │     status:ready
    19|              │      open Task issue    │   │  · execute         │
    20|              │  · for satisfied that   │   │  · commit results  │
    21|              │    were open: close     │   │  · close issue     │
    22|              └─────────────┬───────────┘   └─────────┬──────────┘
    23|                            │                          │
    24|                            └──────► GitHub Issues ◀───┘
    25|                                  (task queue + audit log)
    26|```
    27|
    28|- **Vault** is the source of truth — see `../DATA_CONTRACT.md`.
    29|- **PHASE_DAG.yaml** is the contract for which landmarks gate which transitions and which workflow produces each landmark.
    30|- **Tasks** are GitHub Issues in this repo, labeled with case, phase, skill, and status. One open issue per actionable unit of work.
    31|- **Materializer** runs on a cron, walks every open case, and opens new task issues for landmarks that aren't satisfied yet.
    32|- **Worker** is event-triggered (issue labeled `status:ready`), picks up exactly one task, and executes it.
    33|
    34|This is the "Atomic Tasks + Reactive Materializer" architecture from the design discussion, implemented with GitHub Issues as the queue. **No external database. No long-running daemon.** Cron + Issues + agents.
    35|
    36|## Files
    37|
    38|| File | What it is |
    39||---|---|
    40|| `task_schema.md` | Contract for what a Task issue looks like — labels, body frontmatter, lifecycle |
    41|| `materializer_prompt.md` | The prompt the scheduled materializer agent runs on every cron tick |
    42|| `worker_prompt.md` | The prompt the worker agent runs when a `status:ready` task is picked up |
    43|| `task_templates/*.yaml` | One file per landmark template — preconditions, inputs, success check, the prompt the worker should use |
    44|
    45|## Starting it up (pilot)
    46|
    47|1. **Pick 2–3 cases** to pilot. Add `pilot:true` to their case file frontmatter.
    48|2. **Schedule the materializer.** Use the `schedule` skill (or a GitHub Action on cron) to run `materializer_prompt.md` every 15 minutes against the pilot cases. Alternatively, run it manually with the worker on one case.
    49|3. **Watch the issues appear.** GitHub Issues UI is your dashboard.
    50|4. **Review and dispatch.** Hand-approve the first few tasks the materializer opens before letting the worker run them autonomously.
    51|5. **Promote tasks one by one.** When a task is ready to be executed, label it `status:ready`. The worker is triggered.
    52|6. **Iterate.** Tweak preconditions and prompts in `task_templates/` based on what you observe.
    53|
    54|## Promoting from pilot to production
    55|
    56|Once the loop is stable on the pilot cases:
    57|
    58|1. Drop the `pilot:true` filter from the materializer prompt.
    59|2. Increase cron frequency (5 min instead of 15) or move to event-driven (file-watch on the vault → `repository_dispatch`).
    60|3. Migrate the prompts into `.github/workflows/*.md` files using GitHub Agentic Workflows so GitHub itself runs them — no local daemon, no `schedule` skill dependency.
    61|
    62|## What this runtime explicitly does NOT do
    63|
    64|- **Multi-case parallelism.** One worker run = one task on one case. Concurrency comes from running the worker more often, not from parallel execution within a run.
    65|- **Long-running tasks.** A task should be 1–10 minutes of agent work. Anything longer should be decomposed.
    66|- **External API state.** If a tool needs OAuth (DocuSign, Filevine), the credentials live in repo secrets, not in the task itself.
    67|- **Direct case file editing without an issue.** Every change to a case file made by an agent should be traceable to a closed task issue. The issue is the audit log.

{"content": "     1|# Task Schema
     2|
     3|A **Task** is one atomic unit of paralegal work. Each task is represented as a single GitHub Issue in this repo. The issue is the queue entry, the audit log, and the human-in-the-loop touch point all at once.
     4|
     5|## Identity
     6|
     7|Each task issue has:
     8|
     9|| Element | Where it lives | Required | Notes |
    10||---|---|---|---|
    11|| **Issue title** | Issue title | yes | Format: `[<phase>] <case-slug>: <skill> — <short summary>` |
    12|| **Issue body** | First post | yes | YAML frontmatter (see below) + a markdown body |
    13|| **Labels** | Issue labels | yes | See "Label vocabulary" |
    14|| **Comments** | Issue comments | no | Used by workers to log progress and by humans to give instructions |
    15|| **Linked PR** | `Closes` reference | no | If the task produced a PR (e.g. drafting a document) |
    16|
    17|## Issue body frontmatter (canonical)
    18|
    19|```yaml
    20|---
    21|task_id: <stable slug, e.g. dest-adkins-demand-draft-001>
    22|case_slug: destiny-adkins
    23|phase: phase_3_demand
    24|landmark: demand_drafted              # the PHASE_DAG.yaml landmark this task aims to satisfy
    25|skill: demand-letter-generation       # the Skills/<slug>/SKILL.md to load
    26|template: task_templates/draft-demand.yaml  # the task template that produced this issue
    27|status: ready                         # ready | claimed | in_progress | needs_review | done | failed | blocked
    28|priority: normal                      # high | normal | low
    29|created_at: "2026-04-07T13:00:00Z"
    30|created_by: materializer              # materializer | human
    31|depends_on: []                        # list of other task_ids that must be done first
    32|inputs:                               # task-specific inputs the worker needs
    33|  case_file: cases/destiny-adkins/destiny-adkins.md
    34|  due_by: "2026-05-01"
    35|success_check: |                      # a vault predicate that becomes true when this task is done
    36|  case.has_document("demand")
    37|---
    38|```
    39|
    40|## Label vocabulary
    41|
    42|**Routing labels** (every task should have one of each):
    43|
    44|- `case:<slug>` — which matter
    45|- `phase:<phase_n_name>` — which phase per `PHASE_DAG.yaml`
    46|- `skill:<slug>` — which skill should run it
    47|- `status:<lifecycle>` — see below
    48|- `priority:<high|normal|low>`
    49|
    50|**Status labels** (exactly one):
    51|
    52|- `status:ready` — the worker may pick this up
    53|- `status:claimed` — a worker has begun (used to prevent double-pickup)
    54|- `status:in_progress` — actively running
    55|- `status:needs_review` — worker finished, awaiting human approval before downstream tasks unlock
    56|- `status:done` — closed, success_check satisfied
    57|- `status:failed` — closed, success_check not satisfied; see failure comment
    58|- `status:blocked` — depends_on not yet done, OR external info needed
    59|
    60|**Optional flags**:
    61|
    62|- `auto` — worker may complete and close without human approval (safe deterministic tasks)
    63|- `review` — worker must stop after producing artifact and apply `status:needs_review`
    64|- `recurring` — produced by a recurring template (e.g. bi-weekly check-in) and should not be closed permanently
    65|- `pilot` — only operate on cases with `pilot:true` in case frontmatter (used while bringing the runtime up)
    66|
    67|## Lifecycle
    68|
    69|```
    70|   materializer creates issue  ────►  status:ready (or status:blocked if depends_on unmet)
    71|
    72|         │
    73|         │  (worker triggered by status:ready)
    74|         ▼
    75|
    76|   status:claimed  ─►  status:in_progress  ─► success_check?
    77|                                                  │
    78|                              ┌───────────────────┼───────────────────┐
    79|                              ▼                   ▼                   ▼
    80|                          true,           true,             false
    81|                          auto label      review label
    82|                              │                 │                   │
    83|                              ▼                 ▼                   ▼
    84|                          status:done   status:needs_review     status:failed
    85|                                                  │
    86|                                          (human flips label)
    87|                                                  ▼
    88|                                            status:done
    89|```
    90|
    91|A task is **closed** only on `status:done` or `status:failed`. `needs_review` is open with the agent waiting on a human label change.
    92|
    93|## Idempotency
    94|
    95|Materializer runs every N minutes. To avoid creating duplicates:
    96|
    97|1. Each task template has a `task_id_template` that produces a deterministic id from `(case_slug, landmark, optional discriminator)`.
    98|2. Before opening an issue, the materializer searches for an open issue with the same `task_id` in its frontmatter. If one exists, it skips.
    99|3. For recurring templates, the discriminator is the time bucket (e.g. ISO week) so the next bucket gets a new id.
   100|
   101|## Dependencies
   102|
   103|Tasks express ordering with `depends_on: [task_id, …]`. The materializer:
   104|
   105|- Will not flip a `status:blocked` task to `status:ready` until every `depends_on` task is `status:done`.
   106|- Will close a `status:blocked` task as `status:failed` if any `depends_on` task closes as `status:failed`, with a comment explaining why.
   107|
   108|GitHub's native `blocks #N` / `blocked by #N` keywords can be used in addition for the GitHub UI's benefit, but the runtime authoritatively reads `depends_on` from the task frontmatter.
   109|
   110|## Worker outputs
   111|
   112|When a worker runs a task it should:
   113|
   114|1. Comment on the issue with a one-paragraph "what I did" summary.
   115|2. Commit any vault changes in a single commit whose message references the task: `task <task_id>: <summary>`.
   116|3. Re-run the `success_check` predicate; flip status accordingly.
   117|4. If `status:needs_review`, leave a checklist in the comment of what the human should verify.
   118|5. Never close the issue without first either marking `status:done` or `status:failed`.
   119|
   120|## Failure handling
   121|
   122|A failed task:
   123|
   124|1. Stays open with `status:failed`.
   125|2. Comment explains the failure (error message, what was attempted, what's missing).
   126|3. Materializer will not retry automatically — failure is sticky.
   127|4. Human re-labels to `status:ready` to retry, or closes manually.
   128|
   129|## Open extensions (not in v0)
   130|
   131|- Sub-tasks via GitHub's "convert to checklist item" — possible later if individual tasks become too coarse.
   132|- Cross-case dependencies — currently every task is scoped to one case.
   133|- Task SLAs / due dates as a separate label namespace (`due:<YYYY-MM-DD>`).

     4|  End-to-end management of liens on a personal injury matter. Identify potential
     5|  liens during treatment, classify by governing law, request final amounts after
     6|  settlement, negotiate reductions, and process payment from settlement funds.

    12|  - Write

    14|  - Grep
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Lien Management
    18|
    19|A single skill spanning the full lien lifecycle. The right action depends on **case phase** and **lien type**, not on which lien skill the user happened to invoke. Use the stage map below to find the right step, then load the type-specific reference for the legal substance.
    20|
    21|## Stage map
    22|
    23|| Case phase | Action | Vault touchpoints |
    24||---|---|---|
    25|| Treatment (Phase 2) — early | **Identify** potential liens as providers and payers become known | Add bullets to `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md` `## Liens` section as `[[liens/<creditor-slug>\|<Creditor>]] — <type> (identified)` and create `cases/<slug>/liens/<creditor-slug>.md` stub |
    26|| Demand (Phase 3) | **Estimate** lien exposure for the demand calculation | Update lien stub frontmatter `estimated_amount`; sum into `## Damages` calc |
    27|| Settlement (Phase 5) | **Request final amounts** from each lien holder; place expected payoff in trust | Update lien stub `final_amount_requested_date`; add Activity Log entry |
    28|| Lien (Phase 6) | **Negotiate** reductions where allowed; **pay** liens; **release** funds | Update lien stub `negotiated_amount`, `paid_date`, `status: paid` |
    29|| Closed (Phase 8) | **Verify** zero outstanding liens; document final | Confirm `case.liens.where(status="outstanding").count == 0` |
    30|
    31|The PHASE_DAG (`workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml`) is the source of truth for which landmarks gate which transition; this skill produces those landmarks.
    32|
    33|## Lien-type decision tree
    34|
    35|```
    36|Did the client receive medical care?
    37|├── Yes
    38|│   ├── Government payer involved?
    39|│   │   ├── Medicare → references/medicare.md
    40|│   │   ├── Medicaid → references/medicaid.md
    41|│   │   └── Tricare/VA → treat like Medicare with VA-specific contacts
    42|│   ├── ERISA self-funded health plan?
    43|│   │   └── Yes → references/erisa.md (subrogation analysis is critical)
    44|│   ├── State-law health insurance (fully-insured)?
    45|│   │   └── Yes → references/providers.md §"Insurer Subrogation" + state anti-subrogation rules
    46|│   └── Hospital or provider lien?
    47|│       └── Yes → references/providers.md
    48|└── No → no medical liens; check for child support, tax, or workers' comp liens (see references/lien-type-contacts.md)
    49|```
    50|
    51|## Quickstart by phase
    52|
    53|### Phase 2 — Identify
    54|1. Read `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md` `## Medical Providers` and `## Insurance Claims`.
    55|2. For each provider, ask whether health insurance was billed; for each carrier, identify the plan type (private, Medicare/Medicaid, ERISA).
    56|3. For each potential lien, create `cases/<slug>/liens/<creditor-slug>.md` with frontmatter:
    57|   ```yaml
    58|   schema_version: 2
    59|   lien_type: medicare | medicaid | erisa | provider | other
    60|   holder: <Creditor Display Name>
    61|   status: identified
    62|   identified_date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
    63|   ```
    64|4. Add a bullet under `## Liens` in the case file: `- [[liens/<creditor-slug>|<Creditor>]] — <type> (identified)`
    65|5. Per `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §3, never rebuild the bullet list — append/edit only.
    66|
    67|### Phase 5 — Request final amounts
    68|1. For each lien with `status != paid`, send the appropriate final-amount request letter from `Templates/`:
    69|   - Medicare → `Templates/final-lien-request.docx` (CMS process; see references/medicare.md)
    70|   - Medicaid → state-specific letter (see references/medicaid.md)
    71|   - ERISA → `Templates/final-lien-request.docx` adapted (see references/erisa.md)
    72|   - Provider → `Templates/final-lien-request.docx`
    73|2. Update lien stub: `final_amount_requested_date`, `status: amount_requested`.
    74|3. Log the activity: `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/<YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM>-correspondence.md` per `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §5.
    75|
    76|### Phase 6 — Negotiate, pay, release
    77|1. When a final amount arrives, set `final_amount` and `final_amount_received_date` on the lien stub.
    78|2. Decide negotiable vs not (see `references/reduction-strategies.md` and `references/compromise-waiver.md`):
    79|   - Medicare: limited; use compromise/waiver process (references/medicare.md §Compromise)
    80|   - Medicaid: state-dependent; common-fund and made-whole arguments
    81|   - ERISA: depends on plan language; check Sereboff/US Airways v. McCutchen analysis in references/erisa.md
    82|   - Provider: usually negotiable; check state hospital lien statute caps in references/providers.md
    83|3. After negotiation, update stub: `negotiated_amount`, `negotiation_date`, `status: negotiated`.
    84|4. Issue payment from trust; record `paid_date`, `paid_amount`, `status: paid`.
    85|5. Obtain release from lienholder; attach to `cases/<slug>/documents/`.
    86|
    87|## Outputs
    88|
    89|- New / updated `cases/<slug>/liens/<creditor-slug>.md` files (one per lien)
    90|- Updated `## Liens` section in `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md` (bullet list)
    91|- Drafted/sent letters in `cases/<slug>/documents/` (filled from `Templates/`)
    92|- Activity log entries documenting requests, calls, negotiations, payments
    93|- Frontmatter status flips that satisfy PHASE_DAG landmarks: `outstanding_liens_identified`, `final_amounts_requested`, `final_amounts_received`, `liens_negotiated`, `liens_paid`
    94|
    95|## References (load only what you need)
    96|
    97|- [`references/medicare.md`](references/medicare.md) — CPN/CPL process, MSPRC contacts, compromise/waiver, common reduction tactics
    98|- [`references/medicaid.md`](references/medicaid.md) — state-by-state notes, Ahlborn arguments, common-fund doctrine
    99|- [`references/erisa.md`](references/erisa.md) — plan language analysis, Sereboff/McCutchen, common-fund and made-whole arguments
   100|- [`references/providers.md`](references/providers.md) — hospital lien statutes, balance billing rules, LOP enforcement
   101|- [`references/reduction-strategies.md`](references/reduction-strategies.md) — generic negotiation playbook (cross-type)
   102|- [`references/compromise-waiver.md`](references/compromise-waiver.md) — legal grounds for compromise
   103|- [`references/lien-type-contacts.md`](references/lien-type-contacts.md) — phone, fax, address cheat sheet by lien type and carrier
   104|
   105|## What this skill does NOT do
   106|
   107|- **Disbursement and trust accounting** — handled by `settlement-statement` and `supplemental-statement` skills.
   108|- **Initial intake of payer info** — that's `case-file-organization` and `medical-records-request`.
   109|- **Drafting the demand** — `demand-letter-generation` consumes the lien estimates produced here but is its own skill.
   110|", "total_lines": 109, "file_size": 6808, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    23|
    24|## Sizing the counter
    25|
    26|The counter amount depends on the gap and the current round:
    27|
    28|| Gap % of demand | First counter | Subsequent counters |
    29||---|---|---|
    30|| > 75% (very low offer) | 10–15% reduction from demand | match their movement, then step down |
    31|| 50–75% | 15–25% reduction from demand | 8–12% per round |
    32|| 25–50% | 25–35% reduction | 5–8% per round |
    33|| < 25% | evaluate for acceptance or split | 2–5% or final |
    34|
    35|Movement should shrink round over round so the adjuster reads the signal that we are approaching our floor. `references/counter-strategies.md` covers first-counter calculation, stalled negotiations, near-settlement closing moves, and the justification language that goes with each.
    36|
    37|## Tactics
    38|
    39|`references/tactics.md` covers anchoring, bracketing, concession patterns, responses to lowball offers, "final offer" pushback, and silence as a tool. Pull from that reference when building the talking points — don't restate it here.
    40|

    42|
    43|Draft the letter as markdown at `cases/<slug>/documents/counter-<round>-<YYYY-MM-DD>.md` with these sections: acknowledge their offer, explain why it's insufficient (tie back to documented specials, liability, and comparable verdicts from `offer-evaluation`), state the counter amount with a concrete justification, set a response deadline (usually 14 days), closing. Use the letterhead reference from `Templates/letterhead.docx` — do not copy the template, just reference it.
    44|
    45|There is no firm-approved counter-offer docx template at this time. The letter is authored as markdown and later formatted for send by the paralegal.
    46|
    47|## Talking points and follow-up
    48|
    49|In the same activity log entry that records the counter being sent, list three or four talking points the attorney can use if the adjuster calls, plus an anticipated-response tree (accept / counter-counter at $X / reject / silence) with the action for each branch. Calendar the follow-up: check in at 7 days if no response, phone call at 14, escalate to supervisor at 21.
    50|
    51|## Outputs

    54|- New entry appended to the `offers` list on the claim file via `offer-tracking` (this skill does not write there directly — it hands the counter amount back to `offer-tracking` to log)
    55|- Activity log entry at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/<YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM>-legal.md` with strategy analysis, talking points, and follow-up schedule
    56|- Refreshed calendar entries for follow-up dates

    61|- [`references/counter-strategies.md`](references/counter-strategies.md) — counter-amount math, letter structure, situation-specific templates, common mistakes

     3|description: >
     4|  Prepare the initial settlement statement for a Phase 5 matter: compute the
     5|  gross-to-net distribution (attorney fee, case costs, lien holdback, net to
     6|  client) and draft the authorization-to-settle document the client signs.
     7|  Produces the `settlement_statement_prepared` landmark in PHASE_DAG.yaml.
     8|allowed-tools:
     9|  - Read

    12|  - Glob
    13|  - Grep
    14|---
    15|
    16|# Settlement Statement
    17|
    18|The first accounting document produced after an offer is accepted. It shows the client exactly what the gross settlement becomes after attorney fee, case costs, and lien holdback — and it travels with the authorization-to-settle that the client signs.
    19|
    20|## When to use
    21|
    22|Phase 5 is active (`status: settlement`), an offer has been accepted (`case.frontmatter.settlement_status == "agreed"`), and the gross amount is fixed. If you are still negotiating, use `offer-evaluation` instead. For the *final* distribution after liens are paid, use `supplemental-statement`.
    23|
    24|## Inputs to gather
    25|
    26|Read `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md` first, then pull:
    27|
    28|- **Gross settlement** — from `## Insurance Claims` or the accepted-offer activity log entry
    29|- **Fee agreement terms** — from `cases/<slug>/documents/legal/` (signed fee agreement). See `references/fee-calculation.md` for pre- vs. post-litigation rates, staged rates, referral splits
    30|- **Case costs** — from the `## Expenses` table in the case file
    31|- **Lien holdback** — sum of `final_amount` (or `estimated_amount` if final is still pending) across every file in `cases/<slug>/liens/` where `status != paid`. Coordinate with `lien-management`.
    32|
    33|## Calculation
    34|
    35|```
    36|gross
    37|  − attorney_fee        (gross × fee_rate; pre-lit 33.33%, post-lit 40% unless agreement differs)
    38|  − case_costs          (itemized from ## Expenses)
    39|  − lien_holdback       (sum of outstanding liens, held in trust)
    40|= net_to_client
    41|```
    42|
    43|Kentucky: fee is calculated on the gross, not on net after liens (KRPC 1.5, standard contingency). Always reconcile against the signed fee agreement; flag any discrepancy to the attorney before drafting.
    44|
    45|Trust handling per KRPC 1.15 — deposit to IOLTA, wait for clearance, pay liens before disbursing to client. See `references/trust-requirements.md`.
    46|
    47|## Outputs
    48|
    49|Write into `cases/<slug>/documents/legal/`:
    50|
    51|- `settlement-statement-<YYYY-MM-DD>.md` — full distribution breakdown (see `local-templates/settlement-statement.md` for the skeleton)
    52|- `authorization-to-settle-<YYYY-MM-DD>.md` — one-page authorization the client signs to approve the distribution (see `local-templates/authorization-to-settle.md`)
    53|
    54|Then:
    55|
    56|- Add an Activity Log entry under `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/<YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM>-legal.md` per `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §5
    57|- This satisfies the PHASE_DAG landmark `settlement_statement_prepared`. When the client signs, the `authorization_to_settle_prepared` and `client_authorized` landmarks follow — route that signing step through `docusign-send`.

    64|- [`references/trust-requirements.md`](references/trust-requirements.md) — KRPC 1.15, IOLTA rules, disbursement order, disputed-lien holds

     5|  costs, and liens, compare to demand and comparable verdicts, and produce

    15|
    16|# Offer Evaluation
    17|
    18|Answers "is this offer any good, and what would the client actually receive?" for a specific offer already logged by `offer-tracking`. If no offer has been logged, stop — run `offer-tracking` first.
    19|
    20|## Inputs
    21|
    22|Read `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md` and the relevant `cases/<slug>/claims/bi-<carrier-slug>.md` to pull: demand amount and date, current offer (amount, date, conditions, deadline), policy limits if known, computed specials from the `damages-calculation` block, fee agreement percentage (default 33⅓% pre-litigation / 40% post-filing; check the actual fee agreement if present in `cases/<slug>/documents/`), case costs to date, and current lien estimates from `cases/<slug>/liens/*.md`.
    23|
    24|## Net-to-client calculation
    25|
    26|```
    27|Net = Gross Settlement − Attorney Fee − Case Costs − Liens
    28|```
    29|
    30|`references/net-calculation.md` covers fee-agreement variations, lien types to include, worked examples, and the best-case / worst-case range you should present when lien amounts are estimated rather than final.

    33|
    34|Contrast the offer against the demand (gap, offer as % of demand), against computed specials (is the offer even covering the economic damages?), and against policy limits if known.     37|

    15|
    16|# Service of Process
    17|
    18|Get the summons and complaint into the defendant's hands (or a statutorily-valid substitute), file proof, and calendar the answer deadline. This skill covers everything from picking the method to satisfying the `service_completed` landmark in `workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml`.

    23|
    24|## Pick the method
    25|
    26|| Defendant | First choice | Fallback |
    27||---|---|---|
    28|| Individual in Kentucky | Sheriff personal service (CR 4.04) | Certified mail restricted delivery (CR 4.01) |
    29|| Individual out of state | Personal service via process server | Secretary of State under long-arm (CR 4.04(7)) |
    30|| Kentucky corporation | Registered agent | Officer or managing agent |
    31|| Foreign corporation | Registered agent in Kentucky | Secretary of State |
    32|| Defendant cannot be located after diligent search | Warning order (CR 4.05) | — |
    33|| Jefferson Co., sheriff has failed repeatedly | Special Bailiff by court order | — |
    34|
    35|Full rule text, corporate-agent lookup, Special Bailiff procedure, and problem-resolution table are in [`references/service-methods.md`](references/service-methods.md).
    36|

    38|
    39|1. Read `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md` to identify every defendant and their last-known address. Read `cases/<slug>/contacts/` for any prior address research.
    40|2. Pick a method per defendant from the table above.
    41|3. Draft/send instructions to the sheriff, process server, or certified-mail submission. Track the 90-day service window from filing (CR 4.01(2)) — miss it and the case is dismissible.
    42|4. Log each attempt as `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/<YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM>-legal.md` with defendant, date, method, result.
    43|5. When service succeeds, file proof of service with the court. Format requirements per defendant type are in [`references/proof-of-service.md`](references/proof-of-service.md).
    44|6. Update the case file: add a `## Service` section (if not present) with a bullet per defendant showing served date, method, and answer deadline. Add the served date to the matching defendant entry so the `service_completed` landmark predicate (`all(d.served_date for d in case.defendants)`) can flip true.
    45|7. Calendar the answer deadline (20 days from personal service / certified delivery; longer for Secretary of State; per court order for warning order).
    46|
    47|## Outputs
    48|
    49|- Per-defendant service attempts and results in the activity log
    50|- Filed proof of service for each served defendant (copy in `cases/<slug>/documents/`)

     3|description: >
     4|  Log settlement offers and counter-offers as they arrive during Phase 4
     5|  negotiation. Maintains the authoritative offer history on the BI claim
     6|  file and produces the initial_offer_received landmark. Upstream of
     7|  offer-evaluation and negotiation-strategy.

    14|---
    15|
    16|# Offer Tracking
    17|
    18|The bookkeeping layer for negotiation. Every incoming offer and every outgoing counter gets appended to the BI claim file so downstream skills (`offer-evaluation`, `negotiation-strategy`, `mediation-prep`) all read from the same history. Separate by design from evaluation — this skill only captures what happened, never decides whether the offer is good.
    19|
    20|## Where offers live
    21|
    22|All offers for a claim live inside `cases/<slug>/claims/bi-<carrier-slug>.md` (or `um-…` / `uim-…` for uninsured / underinsured) as a YAML list in frontmatter. The first offer the skill logs against a claim also flips `case.frontmatter.initial_offer_received = true` on the case file, satisfying the PHASE_DAG `initial_offer_received` landmark.
    23|
    24|Example frontmatter on the claim file:
    25|
    26|```yaml
    27|---
    28|schema_version: 2
    29|claim_type: BI
    30|carrier: State Farm
    31|claim_number: SF-2024-123456
    32|policy_limits: 100000
    33|demand_sent_date: "2024-05-01"
    34|demand_amount: 100000
    35|negotiation_status: active
    36|offers:
    37|  - round: 1
    38|    type: initial_offer
    39|    from: insurance
    40|    date: "2024-06-01"
    41|    amount: 25000
    42|    deadline: null
    43|    conditions: null
    44|    adjuster_notes: "Initial evaluation based on specials only"
    45|    communication_method: letter
    46|    status: countered
    47|  - round: 1
    48|    type: counter
    49|    from: plaintiff
    50|    date: "2024-06-05"
    51|    amount: 85000
    52|    reasoning: "Based on comparable verdicts"
    53|    communication_method: letter
    54|    status: responded
    55|---
    56|```
    57|
    58|Append new entries in order; never edit historical rows. `references/tracking-fields.md` has the full field list, enum values, valid state transitions, and common errors to avoid.
    59|
    60|## Logging an offer
    61|
    62|Read the case file and the relevant claim file first. Determine the round number (increment when a fresh offer-counter pair begins) and the entry type (`initial_offer`, `revised_offer`, `counter`, `final_offer`, `acceptance`, `rejection`). Append the YAML entry to the `offers` list with every required field populated and an activity-log entry at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/<YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM>-correspondence.md` describing how the offer arrived (email, phone, letter) with any attached conditions.
    63|
    64|If this is the first offer on the claim, also set `initial_offer_received: true` in the case file frontmatter.
    65|
    66|When a later event changes the status of an earlier entry (a counter goes out, an offer expires, something gets accepted), edit the existing entry's `status` field rather than duplicating the row.
    67|
    68|## Metrics to compute
    69|
    70|After logging, refresh the running metrics block on the claim file:
    71|
    72|```yaml
    73|metrics:
    74|  gap: <our position − their position>
    75|  movement_them: <latest offer − previous offer>
    76|  movement_us: <previous counter − latest counter>
    77|  days_negotiating: <today − demand_sent_date>
    78|  total_rounds: <max round>
    79|  offer_vs_limits: <pct>
    80|  offer_vs_demand: <pct>
    81|```
    82|
    83|These are the inputs `negotiation-strategy` relies on to plan the next move and that `mediation-prep` drops into the mediation brief.
    84|
    85|## Outputs
    86|
    87|- Appended offer entry in `cases/<slug>/claims/<type>-<carrier-slug>.md` frontmatter
    88|- Refreshed `metrics` block on the same file

    21|

    29|

    31|Q1: Is the client on the vehicle's TITLE?

    36|

    38|    Yes → Vehicle's insurer = PIP
    39|    No  → Q3
    40|
    41|Q3: Does the CLIENT have their own auto insurance?
    42|    Yes → Client's insurer = PIP
    43|    No  → Q4
    44|
    45|Q4: Does a HOUSEHOLD MEMBER have auto insurance?
    46|    Yes → Household member's insurer = PIP
    47|    No  → Kentucky Assigned Claims (KAC)
    48|```

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Mediation Prep
    18|
    19|A single skill for the whole mediation arc — brief writing and authority discussion up front, session tactics in the middle, outcome documentation after. Most cases mediate during Phase 4 (pre-suit) or Phase 7 (post-suit, before trial); the substance of the brief changes but the workflow does not.
    20|
    21|## Stage map
    22|
    23|| Stage | When | Primary output |
    24||---|---|---|
    25|| **Pre-session prep** | ≥ 2 weeks before mediation | Mediation brief + damages summary + authority memo |
    26|| **Session execution** | Day of mediation | Round-by-round notes, counter decisions, mediator's-proposal handling |
    27|| **Post-session documentation** | Within 24 hours | Outcome memo, settlement agreement draft or impasse log, landmark flips |
    28|
    29|## Pre-session prep
    30|
    31|Ground yourself in the case file: `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md`, the full offers history on `cases/<slug>/claims/<type>-<carrier-slug>.md`, the computed specials block written by `damages-calculation`, and any prior offer analyses from `offer-evaluation`. Pull liability proof and medical records from `cases/<slug>/documents/`.
    32|
    33|Draft the mediation brief at `cases/<slug>/documents/mediation-brief-<YYYY-MM-DD>.md` using the structure in `references/mediation-brief-template.md`: introduction, factual background, liability analysis (be honest about weaknesses — mediators see through puffery), damages summary, settlement history, demand with justification, conclusion. The damages summary uses the format in `references/damage-summary.md` — medical by provider and by category, future medical if applicable, past and future lost wages, pain-and-suffering analysis via multiplier or per-diem.
    34|
    35|Before the session, walk the client through `references/settlement-authority.md`: case-value range (best/likely/worst), net-to-client scenarios at several settlement amounts, and three authority levels (opening demand, target, walk-away). Capture the walk-away number in writing — log it in a `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/<YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM>-meeting.md` entry, not in the brief itself.
    36|
    37|## Session execution
    38|
    39|Opening: present case strengths, damage documentation, and a reasonable but anchored demand. Throughout the session: for each offer, compute net to client, compare to authority, advise the client in caucus, develop the counter. Use bracketing to control the settlement midpoint, conditional moves ("I'll go to $X if they go to $Y") to test flexibility, and the mediator's proposal when you're close but stuck. `references/negotiation-tactics.md` covers these moves along with impasse-breaking tactics and defense patterns to anticipate.
    40|
    41|Keep a running round-by-round note during the session. If you need additional authority mid-session, discuss it with the client in caucus using the factors in `references/settlement-authority.md` — certainty vs. risk, emotional cost of trial, defense posture, case weaknesses revealed.
    42|
    43|## Post-session documentation
    44|
    45|If the case settled, document: total amount, payment terms, release scope, confidentiality terms, dismissal terms, any special provisions. Update `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md` frontmatter: `settlement_status: agreed`, `settlement_amount`, `settlement_date`. This satisfies the `settlement_reached` landmark and the `mediation_attempted` landmark if in Phase 7. Log a `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/<YYYY-MM-DD-HHMM>-legal.md` entry with the outcome. The settlement processing handoff goes to `settlement-statement`.
    46|
    47|If the case reached impasse, document final positions on both sides, reasons, and any continued negotiation terms (leave-the-door-open language, mediator's-proposal follow-up, next scheduled touch). Set `settlement_status: impasse` on the case frontmatter (satisfies `impasse_declared`, transitions Phase 4 → Phase 7) or log the mediation attempt in Phase 7 without changing status if litigation was already underway.
    48|
    49|## Outputs
    50|
    51|- Mediation brief: `cases/<slug>/documents/mediation-brief-<YYYY-MM-DD>.md`
    52|- Damages summary: either embedded in the brief or as `cases/<slug>/documents/mediation-damages-<YYYY-MM-DD>.md`
    53|- Authority memo: activity log entry (meeting) capturing walk-away number
    54|- Session notes: activity log entry (legal) with round-by-round history
    55|- Outcome: activity log entry (legal) plus frontmatter flags satisfying `settlement_reached` / `impasse_declared` / `mediation_attempted` landmarks
    56|- If settled: handoff to `settlement-statement`
    57|
    58|## References
    59|
    60|- [`references/mediation-brief-template.md`](references/mediation-brief-template.md) — full brief format, section-by-section guidance, what not to include
    61|- [`references/damage-summary.md`](references/damage-summary.md) — medical by provider / category, future medical, wage calculation, pain-and-suffering frameworks
    62|- [`references/negotiation-tactics.md`](references/negotiation-tactics.md) — session tactics: anchoring, bracketing, mediator's proposal, impasse-breaking, defense patterns
    63|- [`references/settlement-authority.md`](references/settlement-authority.md) — client authority discussion, worksheet, mid-session authority requests, ethical considerations
    64|
    65|## What this skill does NOT do
    66|
    67|- **Plan the pre-mediation counter-offer exchange** — that's `negotiation-strategy`.
    68|- **Draft the settlement statement and disbursement** — that's `settlement-statement` and `supplemental-statement` after settlement is reached.
    69|- **Trial prep** — if mediation fails and the case heads to trial, see `trial-presentation` and related litigation skills.

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Medical Chronology (Ongoing)
    18|
    19|Combines visit-level extractions from `cases/<slug>/documents/_extractions/` into a single narrative chronology plus a structured visits table. This skill is the cheap, standalone version of chronology — use it when you need an up-to-date timeline and the extractions are already on disk.
    20|
    21|## Prerequisites
    22|
    23|Extraction reports exist under `cases/<slug>/documents/_extractions/` (produced by `document-processing` or `medical-records-comprehensive-analysis` Phase 2). If they do not, call `document-processing` first or run the comprehensive analysis skill.
    24|
    25|## Inputs and outputs
    26|
    27|Reads:
    28|- `cases/<slug>/<slug>.md` — for client name and `date_of_incident`
    29|- `cases/<slug>/documents/_extractions/*.txt` (or `*.md` extraction reports)
    30|- `cases/<slug>/documents/_extractions/case_facts.md` if present (incident context)
    31|
    32|Writes:
    33|- `cases/<slug>/documents/chronology.md` — narrative chronology
    34|- `cases/<slug>/documents/visits_summary.md` — structured visits table
    35|
    36|## How the synthesis works
    37|
    38|Pull every visit and every billing entry out of the extraction reports. Normalize dates to `YYYY-MM-DD` (push unclear dates to end-of-month and flag). Sort chronologically, earliest first. De-duplicate by (date, provider, visit type) — prefer the most complete version and cite both source files when contents conflict. Identify treatment gaps of more than 30 days and mark them; anything over 60 days gets a stronger flag for attorney review. Calculate totals: visits, providers, bill amounts, treatment duration in days relative to `date_of_incident`.
    39|
    40|## Narrative chronology shape
    41|
    42|`chronology.md` leads with a header block (client, case, incident date, treatment window, totals), then one section per visit in date order:
    43|
    44|```markdown
    45|### YYYY-MM-DD — <Provider> — <Visit Type>
    46|
    47|**Chief complaint:** ...
    48|**Exam / findings:** ...
    49|**Diagnoses:** ...
    50|**Treatment:** ...
    51|**Source:** <extraction filename>[, p. N]
    52|```
    53|
    54|Between visits, insert gap markers when the interval exceeds 30 days: `> Treatment gap: 45 days (YYYY-MM-DD to YYYY-MM-DD).` Close the document with a financial summary (total billed, by provider), a gap summary, and a key milestones section (first treatment, imaging, referrals, PT start/end, surgery, MMI if reached).
    55|
    56|## Structured visits table shape
    57|
    58|`visits_summary.md` is a single wide markdown table — one row per visit — with columns for date, day-since-incident, provider, visit type, chief complaint, diagnoses, treatments, bill amount, and source file. Follow with a small stats block (totals, averages, unique providers, gaps > 30 days).
    59|
    60|## Citation and quote rules
    61|
    62|Every row cites the source extraction report. Direct quotes from medical records stay in quotation marks. When two sources conflict, note the discrepancy in the narrative entry and cite both.
    63|
    64|## Landmarks
    65|
    66|No PHASE_DAG landmarks are produced directly by this skill; it's a synthesis output used by demand drafting and deposition prep. The chronology itself does not flip any landmark — the record/bill receipt landmarks come from the per-provider stubs (handled by `medical-records-request`).
    67|
    68|## What this skill does NOT do
    69|
    70|- **Run the full analysis.** Inconsistency detection, red flags, causation analysis, and missing-records work live in `medical-records-comprehensive-analysis`.
    71|- **Extract text from PDFs.** That is `document-processing`.
    72|- **Produce a printable exhibit PDF.** That is `medical-chronology-pdf-export`.
